critique.summary: The article introduces Horizon, a GPU-accelerated terminal application written in Rust that offers an infinite canvas for organizing terminal sessions. It features pan and zoom capabilities, workspace grouping, full terminal emulation, AI agent integration, git status panels, and session persistence.
mainPoints: Horizon provides an infinite 2D canvas to place, resize, and group terminal panels freely.
It supports multiple color-coded workspaces with various layout modes and a minimap for navigation.
The terminal emulation is powered by the Alacritty engine, supporting 24-bit color, mouse reporting, and keyboard protocols.
Horizon integrates AI agents such as Claude Code and Codex with session persistence and usage tracking.
Built-in git integration allows viewing repo status, diffs, and file changes without context switching.
Interactive features include clickable URLs and file paths detected in terminal output.
A live settings editor with YAML syntax highlighting allows instant configuration changes.
Session persistence restores workspaces, panel positions, and terminal history on restart.
The project is open source, written primarily in Rust, and available for Linux, macOS, and Windows.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Horizon's features and capabilities align with the information available on its official GitHub repository, which is a credible source for the project's details. The repository provides documentation, source code, and installation instructions that confirm the described functionalities such as GPU acceleration, infinite canvas, AI agent integration, and git support. There are no apparent factual inaccuracies in the article's description of the software.
considerations: The article does not discuss potential performance impacts or hardware requirements for GPU acceleration.
There is no mention of user feedback, community adoption, or comparative analysis with other terminal emulators.
Security implications of integrating AI agents and clickable terminal output are not addressed.
The stability and maturity of the software given it has no official releases yet are not discussed.
Compatibility details beyond the listed platforms and Rust version requirements could be important for users.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around Horizon, a GPU-accelerated infinite-canvas terminal in Rust. Users share feedback on usability, feature requests like zooming and multi-screen support, and comparisons to past interface paradigms.

comments.author: tekacs
comments.text:
This is fun! I switched to https://github.com/manaflow-ai/cmux for a while, but had to switch back to Ghostty due to its unreliability, high memory and CPU usage and a bunch of bugs.

This makes a lot of sense, but... it'd be great to allow pulling out of a canvas into a second canvas for those of us with multiple screens (you at least end up needing one window per screen).

In general it feels like... more structure rather than less feels like it'd be the smoothest experience. I'll play with your Ctrl+K shortcut and see if it ends up feeling like I can get everywhere that I need quickly.

But... nice work!

Note for jj users like me: you need to `git lfs pull` if you want to `cargo run --release`!

Update: No luck creating any 'shell' workspaces (it looks like you use GNU-only flags to script) – I'll push a fix once I find it.

Also: the AGENTS.md is wrong JFYI - it points to portable-pty, when this is using alacritty_terminal's tty (on rustix-openpty)
comments.analysis: This comment provides detailed, experience-based feedback with practical suggestions and notes on technical issues, making it highly credible and constructive.

comments.author: ms_menardi
comments.text:
This is great! Two things I noticed immediately:

the settings window overlaps with the minimap.

I think it'd be neat if you could zoom? The reason I'd use this over a tiling window manager is to fit more terminals on a screen at once. I think if there was a capability like the Strategic Zoom in Supreme Commander (video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9hJY7exr9KU) it would be much easier to manage many terminals at once, and take advantage of the infinite canvas.
comments.analysis: Offers useful UI/UX observations and a relevant feature idea inspired by a known interface, showing insight into user needs.

comments.author: cadamsdotcom
comments.text:
Maybe I’m old but this sounds like MDI (multiple-document interface) of the late 90s.

Went the way of the dodo probably because it’s hard for non power users to grok.

But this is a power user tool - seeems like a fit!
comments.analysis: Provides historical context and perspective on interface design, which is insightful though somewhat subjective.

comments.author: fritzo
comments.text: I was hoping this would be an infinite length terminal view, like the opening backstory in Star Wars
comments.analysis: This comment is more of a humorous or emotional reaction without substantive technical content or constructive feedback.
